name: seaborn
summary: Statistical data visualization built on matplotlib — beautiful charts with sensible defaults and one-line syntax.

story.problem: matplotlib gives you full power but requires a lot of code for common statistical plots like distributions, correlations, and categorical comparisons. Styling it to look good takes even more effort.
story.mental_model: seaborn is a high-level wrapper around matplotlib. Think of it as matplotlib with pre-built chart types for statistics, better color palettes by default, and tight pandas integration. It still returns matplotlib Figure/Axes objects, so you can drop down to matplotlib for fine-tuning.
story.when_to_use: Exploratory data analysis, correlation matrices, distribution comparisons, categorical plots, regression plots, or any time you want a beautiful statistical chart in a few lines.
story.when_not_to_use: Custom non-statistical visualizations, interactive web charts, or when you need pixel-perfect control over layout.

alternatives.name: matplotlib
alternatives.reason: More control, lower-level, no statistical defaults

nodes.label: seaborn
nodes.summary: Top-level module — most functions live here

nodes.label: sns.heatmap()
nodes.summary: Color-encoded matrix, great for correlation tables

nodes.label: sns.pairplot()
nodes.summary: All pairwise relationships in a dataset at once

nodes.label: sns.lmplot()
nodes.summary: Scatter plot + linear regression line + confidence interval

nodes.label: sns.set_theme()
nodes.summary: Set global style, palette, and font scale

steps.label: Import and compute correlation
steps.code:
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

df = sns.load_dataset('iris')  # or your own DataFrame
corr = df.select_dtypes('number').corr()

steps.label: Plot heatmap
steps.code:
sns.set_theme(style='white')
fig = sns.heatmap(
    corr,
    annot=True,    # show numbers
    fmt='.2f',
    cmap='coolwarm',
    center=0,
    square=True,
    linewidths=0.5
).figure
fig.suptitle('Feature Correlations')
fig.tight_layout()

steps.label: Violin + strip plot combo
steps.code:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

tips = sns.load_dataset('tips')
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.violinplot(data=tips, x='day', y='total_bill', hue='sex', split=True, ax=ax)
sns.stripplot(data=tips, x='day', y='total_bill', hue='sex', dodge=True, size=3, alpha=0.4, ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Bill distribution by day and gender')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
